interpretations.interpretation: The European Union sacred political order demonstrates high corrigibility through its constitutive pluralist architecture: the TEU's 'ever closer union' frames integration as an open-ended process rather than a sacred destination, and its grounding in liberty, democracy, and rule of law provides normative self-correction mechanisms. Unlike the high-pathology cases, the EU's sacred political order cannot generate a sacred enemy from within its own structure — its legitimacy depends on multi-state consensus and legal adjudication. Democratic-backsliding episodes (Hungary, Poland) and Brexit test this corrigibility but also activate the order's correction mechanisms (Article 7, infringement procedures), supporting rather than undermining the corrigibility score.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin. Founding document passages establish the design intent; operational corrigibility requires expansion. Democratic-backsliding episodes introduce uncertainty.

interpretations.mechanismSummary: The EU's multi-state, open-ended integration architecture structurally opposes sacred-enemy escalation and sacrificial demands, producing high corrigibility through consensus-building and legal adjudication rather than symbolic unification around a common threat.

claims.claim: The EU sacred political order institutionalizes pluralist reordering as its constitutive principle: the TEU frames integration as a continuous process ('ever closer union') rather than a fixed final state, and grounds that process in liberty, democracy, and rule of law rather than sacred-enemy construction — a symbolic architecture that produces corrigibility through ongoing multi-state negotiation rather than sacrificial escalation.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Both passages are from the official EUR-Lex consolidated TEU preamble, verified against the authoritative source. The 'ever closer union' recital is foundational to EU self-understanding; the liberty/democracy/rule-of-law recital specifies the sacred political order's normative grounding. Confidence capped: chain requires expansion to include institutional structures (Council, Parliament, Court of Justice) and crisis episodes (Brexit, Hungary, Poland) that complicate the corrigibility score.

passages.locator: Treaty on European Union (consolidated version), Preamble, 4th recital; Official Journal EU C 202, 7 June 2016; text verified at https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:12016ME/TXT
passages.text: CONFIRMING their attachment to the principles of liberty, democracy and respect for human rights and fundamental freedoms and of the rule of law

source.notes: Official EUR-Lex consolidated version. Verified at https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:12016ME/TXT

passages.locator: Treaty on European Union (consolidated version), Preamble, 1st recital; Official Journal EU C 202, 7 June 2016; text verified at https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:12016ME/TXT
passages.text: RESOLVED to continue the process of creating an ever closer union among the peoples of Europe

source.notes: Official EUR-Lex consolidated version. Verified at https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:12016ME/TXT

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Score of 4 reflects high but not maximum corrigibility: the EU's pluralist architecture and legal correction mechanisms are strong, but democratic-backsliding within member states and the Brexit precedent reveal limits to the order's self-correction capacity. Not maximum because the order cannot fully enforce its own normative standards on member states without triggering exit or legitimacy crises.

scores.reviewNote: Score value 4/5 defensible: pluralist-reordering mechanism (Treaty on European Union's consensus architecture) represents high corrigibility. Confidence 0.60 correctly reflects that democratic backsliding in member states and Brexit's demonstration of exit-mechanism limits are genuine threats to the corrigibility claim. Hold at score-review until chain expansion; flag democratic-backsliding cases as a priority secondary source for the chain.
